abstract: This article examines the operational status of the administrative judiciary in Jordan following the transition from a single-level system, formerly the Supreme Court of Justice, to a dual-level structure comprising the Administrative Court and the Supreme Administrative Court. This structural reform significantly bolstered public confidence by ensuring that administrative cases are scrutinized by two distinct judicial bodies, thereby aligning Jordanian practice with international guarantees of fair trial standards. Utilizing an applied empirical approach, this study analyzes statistical data from 2020, revealing a notable increase in caseloads compared to 2019. Despite this progress, the study identifies a critical geographic limitation: first-instance administrative courts remain centralized in the capital, Amman, rather than being accessible throughout the Kingdom's governorates. Consequently, the author argues for the decentralization of the administrative judiciary through the establishment of regional courts of first instance and appeal. Furthermore, the article highlights the necessity of developing clearer jurisdictional standards to distinguish between ordinary and administrative judicial authorities, ensuring a more specialized and accessible legal framework for resolving administrative disputes in Jordan.
